steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what Anbar et al. and Slotznick et al. would most likely disagree about. Anbar et al. conclude atmospheric oxygen rose at 2.5 Ga based on a Mo spike. Slotznick et al. argue oxidative weathering of volcanic debris could have occurred long after deposition via microfractures, so the Mo spike may not reflect oxygen levels at 2.5 Ga.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Is the increase in Mo in that portion attributable to oxidative weathering of the material found in that portion?: Both teams accept that oxidative weathering explains the Mo — they disagree only on when it happened. ✗
  • Choice B — Is the increase in Mo in that portion indicative of an increase in atmospheric oxygen dating to the same time?: Anbar et al. say yes; Slotznick et al. say the weathering could have occurred later, so the Mo spike does not necessarily mark an oxygen rise at 2.5 Ga. This is exactly their disagreement. ✓
  • Choice C — Did chemically analyzing bulk samples lead to a false impression that there is an increase in Mo in that portion?: Slotznick et al. acknowledge the Mo increase is real — they question its timing, not whether it exists. ✗
  • Choice D — Does the increase in Mo in that portion suggest that atmospheric oxygen levels were not negligible before 2.5 Ga?: Neither text focuses on oxygen levels before 2.5 Ga; the dispute is about what the Mo spike means at 2.5 Ga. ✗
B is correct.
